reasoning: There is currently no approved vaccine for histoplasmosis (or any fungal infection in humans). A review of recent literature and clinical trial databases (up to April 2026) shows that research is confined to animal models (such as mice) and immunoinformatics. A recent publication (Nov 2025) explicitly noted that human fungal vaccines remain 'so far off'. Given that developing and approving a novel vaccine typically takes at least 10 years from the onset of phase I clinical trials—and no phase I trials have even begun for histoplasmosis—the probability of an approved vaccine being listed on Wikipedia in the next 1-5 years is virtually zero. By 2036, there is a small chance that mRNA or other novel platforms could expedite the development of a candidate, but the probability remains low. I am setting the near-term probabilities to the minimum bound of 5%, rising slightly by the 2036 horizon.
